telltalePatterns: dentalization of alveolar stops and fricatives (/ð/ to [d̪])
vowel raising of open-front /æ/ towards mid-front [ɛ]

phonemeProblems.produced: [d̪] (dental stop) instead of voiced dental fricative

prosody.intonation: L1-influenced; slightly compressed pitch range with rising-falling contours on focused words
prosody.fluencyNotes: High fluency with natural phrase-boundary pausing; consistent use of linking between 'have-their' and 'trying-to'.

strengths: Excellent production of the American rhotic /ɹ/ in coda positions
Clear distinction between high-front /i/ and /ɪ/ in 'people' and 'lives'
Advanced command of connected speech and elision in 'I'm just'

recommendedFocus.reason: Correcting the dental stop to a fricative in high-frequency words like 'together' and 'their' significantly reduces perceived foreign accent.
Lowering the jaw for /æ/ in words like 'matching' prevents phonemic overlap with /ɛ/ (metching).
Full voicing of /z/ in 'lives' and 'always' improves lexical clarity and prosodic flow.

result.transcript: It's funny how some people always seem to have their lives together while I'm just trying to find my matching socks.
